speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I went up to Bucharest-- took me a couple of days, because of the distance and the transportation. They were not very ideal. And when I got to Budapest, I went also to the Jewish community. And I found out that in Bucharest, they had a viable Jewish community yet. And the people that I worked with in Budapest were also in Bucharest by that time already. And that's when I found out about this Zieman. And from him, a new guy took over, who they brought from Israel, by the name Farcasz. And that Farcasz recruited me to work [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people-- to go back to my hometown and get some people out. Make, make some-- because they said that my hometown will become part of Russia-- to make certificates, so they legally can leave the city. And I did that. And for-- I was there only two days. And then I was warned that this guy that, that gave me trouble before could cause more trouble. So I left. And I just left all these papers that I-- were printed that gave permission to leave our hometown. And I went up to Budapest. And then in Budapest there was-- this Farcasz was there already. And I worked with him for about three months, where I would go with the Jewish people to Austria through the Russian zone, and through to Italy. And I, I think I made about five or six trips. And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this 1945, already?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1945. And then, in May, I went up to Budapest. And first of all, all this time, I was looking for my-- any remnants. And I was hoping, at least, my brother and my sister will come back. I had no such a sign. But then one time, in Budapest, somebody told me that some people came back from Auschwitz and that they saw my sister, and that she, she might be alive. And I went up again. I worked with a group-- I had to go to Bucharest again. I went to Bucharest. I was there for about two days, when I heard that somebody's looking for me. And it turned out to be my sister. [CRYING] That's why I met her in Bucharest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and I asked her, did anybody survive? And she said, no, nobody else survived, and that she will go back to my hometown and pick up the gold that we had. And she did. And she went up, then, to Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went-- I made two trips. I came back from Bucharest again. And I made two trips to Prague. And, and I was shuffling between Prague and Budapest. I did my-- a little of my own business, too, at that time. And then when I got, once, to, to Budapest, I was told by this Farcasz that I have to take one more group to Villach. Villach is a city on the Austro-Italian border. And I took quite a large group. And a lot of them were people that came from my hometown with the papers that I left them to escape. These were some people that came from concentration camps back home. But it was not so easy anymore to leave because the Russians were taking over. And any time you got on a train, you needed a-- what they call [RUSSIAN]. I remember the name till today. And that was-- these were the papers that I brought. And I left it with somebody in my hometown-- that anybody who wants to leave should be able to get this document. And as I said, I went again back to Budapest. And, and I took this group to Villach. And when I came back from Villach, this Farcasz told me that a boat is leaving from Constanta to Israel. And if you'd like to go, you can go over there. And exactly what I did-- I went back to Bucharest, and from Bucharest to Constanta, and from Constanta, on this old, rickety boat-- took me about, I don't know, a week-- we wound up in Israel. Of course, the British arrested us and put us into Atlit. But I wasn't there too long. I was there, oh, two or three days. And then the-- they-- the [NON-ENGLISH], or the local Jewish authorities, arranged a certificate that time they needed. I was able to get a certificate. And I lived in Israel from '45 till-- I had papers to come to the United States already. And in the meantime, my sister was back in Prague. And she got married. And she was going to the United States. And I wanted to go to the United States also. And I got my papers to-- around end of '47. But then the war broke out in Israel. And I was drafted. And I had to stay another-- close to-- almost-- two years in, in, in the army. And I fought in, in the Palmach there for actually about 12 months-- real fighting in Jerusalem, and on the way of the liberation to Jerusalem, and also of the liberation of the old city. And I was one of the first ones to break in there. And one of the reasons I, I became a lieutenant very fast-- because I was able to read Czech. And the weaponry that arrived was-- or mostly Czech. And I had an engineering background. And I learned the use of these weapons. And I was the one who was training many of the people how to use these mortars, machine guns, and rifles, just through Czech instruction manuals-- and service them. And I was in Jerusalem for about-- from, I would say, November of-- oh, end of November-- in December of-- I'm sorry. I take that back-- December of '47 'till July '48. And then, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, is-- I was involved in a lot of the heavy fighting also in the patrols that were patrolling the roads between Jerusalem and, and Gush Etzion, they called it. And I was in these armored vehicles traveling every alternate day, I-- many weeks. And then it fell to the Jordanian Army the day-- on my day off. If it was my day on, I would have been there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came back to Jerusalem. And from there-- and, as I said, is-- it was either June or July-- I got transferred into [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the so-called Israeli Navy that didn't exist. There just existed a few motorboats. But I was transferred to Haifa and I took a, a-- took, I believe, a six-month, six-month course-- an officer's course in navigation. And then-- and I graduated, I believe, in September.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then they had the armistice, you know. It was either August or September they signed the armistice. And the war was over. And then I came back. And unfortunately, I had all my-- when I went back to my hometown, they stole our furniture. But all the pictures, and old documents-- my father's diploma-- everything was thrown into a little barn in our backyard. And I picked up many things. And I was-- be able to be in possession of it for a long time. But then, when I was in Israel, I had to lease an apartment. And under the British laws, if you didn't pay rent for three months, the landlord had the power to move you out. And I was in Jerusalem. And we were surrounded. And I was there for 10 or 12 months. And this man moved out all my belonging. And I lost it then. And I came back. I had nobody to talk to. And then I decided that I don't want to live here, either. And since we applied for papers, I did that. And about six months later, I was on a boat to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived here-- oh, I think it was either April or May, you know, something-- I don't even remember that date.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in 1950, just shortly after I got discharged from the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York. By-- I-- at that time, I came. And I wanted to go to dental school. And-- but I also had to make a living. I had an aunt who was-- I wouldn't say well off, but she was comfortable. And she could have supported me. But she had her own family. And so when I came-- already was after many other cousins came who stayed with her. And they were sort of like old hat. So there was not too much room for me. So I stayed there for about a week or so. And then I got my own apartment. And I started getting a job as a dental technician because that's the thing that I thought I knew best. Because my English was not that-- it was-- I spoke English. I learned English at home yet. But I did-- was not fluent. So I figured, to be fluent, I'll work in this dental laboratory. And I did. But I started getting good at it. And I was making better salaries at that time than even engineers made those days. And I sort of remained with that. In the meantime, I met my wife in 1951. And we got married in June of 1952. And we raised a family in New Jersey. My wife-- I was-- lived in New York. My wife's from New Jersey. She came to Brighton Beach with a friend who was from Israel. And I met people who were also from Israel because I spoke Hebrew at that time. And we spent there the whole day and never even spoken to one another.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the way back, I see this gal who is walking to the train station. And she's asking me, do you think I can get a ride with you to Midtown? I said, I can give you the same ride that you can give me-- that's namely the subway.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, together, we're going on the subway because I lived in Washington Heights in New York. And she lived in Newark, New Jersey. And that's where we met. And before we parted, I asked her if it's OK if I call her for a date. She said she'd love to. She gave me her phone number. And I called her. And we kept on meeting. And it led to marriage in 1952-- June the 1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paul is the oldest-- Paul Bernard. Then Lawrence T is the middle one. And Sharon Debra is the youngest daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do they do today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my older boy is an author. And he published some books. He also writes scripts. And he also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] does many things. He owned a restaurant for quite a while. But he sold that. He owns his own home. And he lives in the Los Angeles area. And right now, he is involved in the production of some kind of a movie. I, I don't really know because-- which one because he did that many times before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does he have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives with this girl. But he's not married.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your younger boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger boy is also unmarried. And he is an-- he's an electronic engineer-- graduate electronic engineer. And he's involved in export and import with Japan. And he does a very good living--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: --also lives in Los Angeles. And my daughter is married with two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the children's name is-- [? Nina ?] is the oldest. And the youngest is [? Tessa Marie-- ?] two lovely children. They live in Berkeley. And they were also in the film and video business till very recently. And now she's working at a forensic outfit, just like you hear on the O.J. Simpson case. She runs an office like that in Berkeley. As a matter of fact, they mentioned her office name in the trial-- I heard it once. And she does a fairly good living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jean, why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why do I think I survived? The will to survive-- I had the will and the drive all the time to survive. And I also made a very good living in this country when I came here. I started getting involved in the-- as I said, is-- I wound up to be a dental technician.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I worked for somebody-- till-- this is in New Jersey. And at the time, he asked me to be his partner because I, I excelled at it. Because I was going to leave and open up my own business. So he said why don't you remain and be my partner? And it was-- we had a very nice relationship for about five years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in '59, I decided to go on my own business anyway. Because although we had a good relation, but-- it was a little too one-sided. And I was running the place. I built it up from two people-- at the time that we had when I started working there-- two people who worked for him and the boss-- to 28 people. And I did all the running of the business-- everything. Talking to the customers, buying merchandise, selling, billing. And he was just sitting in the office and traveling. Because we made quite good money. Then I said to myself that it's a little uneven. I do all the work. And, and, by the way, he owns also the building. And he's charging, in those days, huge rents. Well, I, I, I mean, I don't blame him. But I said to myself, well, if I can do it for him, I can do it for myself. And I worked for the-- I did-- in cosmetic-- those days are in cosmetic dentistry. And I did work for very famous actors at that time. And they liked it in California. And the many dentists from California asked me, why don't you come here because this is the capital of entertainment, where you do this kind of work. And, and, in 1960, we did just that. We sold our home. I had an agreement with my wife. If we-- if we sell the house in 1960, then we move. If can't sell it, then we wouldn't move because there was a little real estate slump. However, the first guy that came to look at the house loved it so much he bought it immediately.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I made all the arrangements. And in July, when the kids got through kindergarten and school, we packed into a car, got our furniture, and we moved to Los Angeles. Tell me something. If-- of your experiences--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as a Jew during World War II--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: --what have you learned that you want to pass on to future generations and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: What have I learned? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, what I have learned is that people are people wherever they are. And they develop meanness through behavior, mainly their-- in mainly religion. That-- they talk about love. They teach hate. And for generations and generations, they were teaching hate and hate. And that hate multiplies itself. And it perpetuates itself. And you find out that good people become haters, just through some of the education. And I think what I learned is to teach my children and my family of tolerance. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And if you're somehow tolerant of your fellow man, you're tolerant of yourself. And it gives you greater latitude to succeed in your private life and in business. And I think that's what happened to me. Because when I moved to California, I became one of the most successful people in cosmetic dentistry. I have patents in, in, in the dental field-- quite a few of them. And I sold my business about eight years ago. And I'm asked to lecture all over the world right now. And if I wouldn't be tolerant of people, I don't believe I could do that. Because I also teach in Germany-- the people that murdered my family. And I look at the people that are there today not as killers, but as children of the people who might have been killers. You, you know, you don't know who was who because I don't ask family certificates from people that I teach with. I do that in-- as a matter of fact, I feel-- in Germany, I don't feel that uncomfortable. I feel very uncomfortable in Vienna because-- and by the way, my father did go to that school. And the dean of the university is a very good friend of mine. And every once in a while, after the lecture, we go out for, for dinner. And I hear these anti-Semitic remarks, even today. I don't hear that in Germany-- maybe there is. But somehow, it doesn't come across. I was in Germany in-- as a matter of fact, I lectured-- what was it, in April, in one of the largest dental meetings that took place in Cologne. And there I met some Swedish people, who asked me to go to Sweden. And I just came back a week ago. And as I said, is-- your question that you asked me before-- if I wouldn't be tolerant, I couldn't be tolerant of myself. And I couldn't forgive myself also that I survived. So therefore, I, I, I go forward in life without hate, without anger. Because hate hurts the man who hates more than, than the one who perpetuates it. And the only thing that I'm sorry about-- that I see so many people who survived the war who were the murderers, who survived, and doing quite well, despite of-- whatever they did. But I'd like to go back to my private business that I had here in, in California from 1960 till 1987. It was about 27 years. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Slowly, I built up a very good business where I had, at one case, 48 employees. And we were the largest dental service in the state of California. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also, as I said, I had patents on equipment and on instruments. I still have it. But it-- they already expired because they're over 17 years. And, and in 1940-- I'm sorry, in 1987-- I decided to sell my business. And I invested some of the money with a landsman of ours who I knew as a lifelong friend, who turned out to be a fraud, unfortunately. And I lost most of my money that I made with him-- that I made with myself. And that is still in trial. And I hope to get some of it back. And, and my hearing is actually the 26th of this month. And that's why I'm home now. I'm hoping that some of the investment that I made-- and what is a very large one-- I will get back because I feel I was defrauded. However, I also know this-- that money never made me. I make money. And whether I'm going to leave this earth with more money, less money, wouldn't make any difference. However, whatever I had made before, I would like to recover whatever I can-- probably will not recover it all. But even if I recover a portion of it all, I'll be quite happy. But even if I don't, I'll be just as happy. And just doing what I'm doing and continue as long as I can to be active.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is a picture of my-- both parents-- my mother, whose maiden name was Sarah Roth. And her married name is Sarah Grunstein. And my father, whose name is Peter Grunstein, or, in Jewish, he was Pinkas Grunstein. And the approximate date of the picture is 1929 or 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czechoslovakia, in my hometown of Velky Sevlus.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is a picture of my sister and myself. It was approximately taken in the year of 1930 in my hometown of Velky Sevlus, Czechoslovakia. And I believe I am, there, either six or seven years old. This is Jean, or [NON-ENGLISH] Grunstein, or Sidi Grunstein, my older sister. This is a family portrait. And I am on the bottom row, the third one from left. And my sister is the fourth one from left. And we are the only two people who survived. This picture was taken also in Velky Sevlus in approximately, I would say, 1928. OK, this is a picture of the house where I was born in 1924, in Velky Sevlus, Czechoslovakia. This picture was taken about a year ago. You can see my wife is in front of it. However, I-- we lived there only for a year after I was born. Then we moved to a much larger home in the center of the city. This is a picture taken in 1948 in Israel, where I was fighting with the Palmach group. The, the picture was taken just outside of Jerusalem-- a place called Castel. I was one of the first ones to break through and, and get a machine-gun position that I was holding up. So the rest of the group could catch up with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Jean Greenstein. And this is, again, around March, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a picture that appeared on the cover of

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture-- one of the pictures that appeared on the cover of the magazine, yes. OK. This is a picture of my immediate family that was taken in 1988.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Encino, California. At that time, I lived in a home. And on top, starting from left, is my son Larry, or Lawrence; myself; my wife; and my older boy, Paul. And those are the parents of my wife, Mr. and Mrs. [? Blumer; ?] and my daughter with her husband and two children-- my daughter Sharon and her daughter, [? Tessa, ?] and also [? Nina. ?] That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Jean, would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Ruth, whose maiden name was [? Blumer. ?] We were married in 1952, 43 years ago. And we have three children-- Paul, Larry, and Sharon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, how-- would you like to say a few words about what was it like to live with a hero of the Holocaust all these years?

speaker: interviewee
text: At times, it was difficult-- in the beginning it was. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I remember when he got up-- excuse me-- and had nightmares. But then he wasn't even fighting the Germans. He was fighting-- in the middle, fighting the Arabs and the Germans. And he was in the middle. So it was quite a conflict there. Being married to a non-American is different, too. I think the ways of a European man are, are different than an American man in, in the helping or, or being part of a family, loving the children, and yet-- and giving them all he can. And yet, there was something that was holding him back from expressing himself, I think, to the children. Being involved in their everyday life, he was. And yet there was a different side. He sat back-- serve me. But things after 43 years have changed quite a bit. And all for the better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you both, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank you. That's it.
